etymology.description: 門(門) (mén, "door") + 一(一) (latch/pole) + 廾(廾) (gǒng, "two hands") -> hands opening a latched door -> 開
etymology.interpretation: The character 開 (kāi, "open") visually represents the action of opening a door with hands, symbolized by combining the door 門(門) with the latch 一 and two hands 廾(廾). This conveys the core idea of opening something secured by a latch.
etymology.simplification: 开 is a visual and structural shorthand of 開, created by merging 一 and 廾 into a simplified form, while retaining the reference to opening a door.

examples.chinese: 老师在上课时总是很开心(老師在上課時總是很開心)。
examples.pinyin: Lǎoshī zài shàngkè shí zǒng shì hěn kāixīn.
examples.english: The teacher is always happy during class.

examples.chinese: 我们要早上开会(我們要早上開會)，讨论计划(討論計劃)。
examples.pinyin: Wǒmen yào zǎoshang kāihuì, tǎolùn jìhuà.
examples.english: We need to have a meeting in the morning to discuss the plan.

examples.chinese: 请把门打开(請把門打開)，让我进去(讓我進去)。
examples.pinyin: Qǐng bǎ mén dǎkāi, ràng wǒ jìnqù.
examples.english: Please open the door and let me in.
